questions.question: What is the defining difference between a B-Tree and a B+ Tree?
options: B+ Trees are binary; B-Trees are not
B+ Trees store all data in leaves and link leaves together; B-Trees store data in every node
B+ Trees only support point lookups; B-Trees support range scans
B+ Trees have lower fanout than B-Trees
questions.explanation: In a B+ Tree, internal nodes only contain routing keys and all data records live in the leaf nodes, which are linked via sibling pointers. This enables efficient range scans. In a B-Tree, data can be stored in any node.

questions.question: Why does high fanout reduce tree height in a B+ Tree?
options: Because high fanout means each key is larger, so fewer fit per page
Because each internal node can have many children, so fewer levels are needed to reach N records
Because the tree rebalances more aggressively
Because fanout is the same as tree height
questions.explanation: Fanout is the number of child pointers per internal node (order + 1). Height is roughly log_{fanout}(N). A fanout of 201 means a tree of 10 million records needs only 3–4 levels.

questions.question: When inserting into a full leaf node in a B+ Tree of order d, what happens?
options: The leaf is discarded and a new leaf is created
The leaf is split in half, the median key is promoted to the parent, and the left leaf's next pointer points to the right leaf
All keys are moved to the parent node
The tree is rebuilt from scratch
questions.explanation: When a leaf exceeds d entries, it splits. The smaller keys stay in the original leaf, the larger keys go to a new leaf, and the smallest key of the new leaf is promoted to the parent as a routing key. The split may cascade upward.

questions.question: With a clustered index on column a and a secondary index on column b, how many B+ Tree probes does SELECT * FROM t WHERE b = 42 require?
options: One probe on the secondary index; rows are stored there directly
Two probes: one on the secondary index to get the primary key, then one on the clustered index to fetch the row
Two probes but they happen in parallel
One probe on the clustered index; the secondary index is ignored
questions.explanation: Secondary indexes store (key, primary_key) pairs. To retrieve the full row, the engine must first find the primary key in the secondary index, then look up the row in the clustered (primary key) index. This is called a double lookup.

questions.question: A composite index is defined on (last_name, first_name). Which of the following queries can use the leftmost prefix of this index?
options: SELECT * WHERE first_name = 'Jane'
SELECT * WHERE last_name = 'Smith' AND first_name = 'Jane'
SELECT * WHERE first_name = 'Jane' AND last_name = 'Smith'
Both B and C
questions.explanation: The leftmost prefix rule says filters must use the leading columns in order. Both B and C filter on last_name first (or include it), so the index can be used. Query A filters only on first_name (not last_name) and cannot use the index.

questions.question: What is the primary reason database B+ Trees use a large order (hundreds of keys per node) instead of a small order like 3 or 4?
options: Large order minimizes the number of disk pages accessed during a lookup
Large order makes inserts faster by reducing split frequency
Large order makes delete simpler
Large order reduces memory usage for internal nodes
questions.explanation: B+ Tree nodes are sized to match disk pages (typically 4–16 KB). A page-sized node with hundreds of keys means internal nodes have huge fanout, so the tree height is 3–4 levels. A lookup touches at most 4 pages regardless of total row count.
